mcqs.question: In the basic CUDA trapezoidal rule implementation, what function is used by each thread to accumulate its f(x_i) contribution into the global result?
mcqs.explanation: Each thread uses atomicAdd(result_p, my_f) to atomically add its computed function value to a single global memory location, ensuring thread-safe accumulation without race conditions.

mcqs.question: What is the primary performance limitation of using atomicAdd for the trapezoidal rule when n is very large?

options.text: atomicAdd serializes concurrent writes to the same address, creating a bottleneck
atomicAdd only works with integer data types
atomicAdd requires shared memory which is limited in size

mcqs.explanation: When thousands of threads all call atomicAdd on the same memory location, the hardware must serialize these operations -- only one thread can complete its atomic update at a time. This makes the accumulation phase O(n) instead of O(n/p), negating the benefit of parallelism.

mcqs.question: In the multi-block CUDA trapezoidal rule kernel, how does a thread compute its global index my_i?
mcqs.explanation: The standard CUDA global index formula is blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x. This produces a unique index for every thread across all blocks, mapping each thread to a specific subinterval point x_i = a + my_i * h.

mcqs.question: Why might the atomicAdd-based CUDA trapezoidal rule produce slightly different results across multiple runs with identical inputs?

options.text: The GPU hardware introduces random noise into computations
Floating-point addition is not associative, and thread execution order varies between runs
The cudaMemset function initializes to different values each time
The number of threads launched changes between runs

mcqs.explanation: Floating-point addition is not associative: (a+b)+c may differ from a+(b+c) in the least significant bits. Since atomicAdd processes threads in non-deterministic order depending on hardware scheduling, the same inputs can produce slightly different sums across runs.

mcqs.question: What must the host code do after the kernel computes the accumulated sum to obtain the final integral value?

options.text: Add f(a) and f(b) to the result
Take the square root of the result

mcqs.explanation: The kernel accumulates the weighted sum f(a)/2 + f(x_1) + f(x_2) + ... + f(b)/2. The trapezoidal rule formula requires multiplying this sum by h = (b-a)/n to obtain the final integral approximation. This multiplication is done on the host after copying the result back from the device.
